title: Excess nitrogen from croplands
subtitle: "Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.
caption:
Excess nitrogen from croplands

"Excess nitrogen" is the difference between nutrient inputs (from fertilizers, manure, and fixation from legumes) and the amount harvested in crop material. This represents nitrogen that are lost to the environment and can create ecological imbalances on ecosystems and in water bodies.

The choropleth map displays the total mass of excess nitrogen from croplands for the year 2009, utilizing a continuous color scale that ranges from light yellow for lower values to dark brown for the highest values. The legend defines the metric in tonnes, with specific thresholds marked at 100,000 tonnes, 250,000 tonnes, 1 million tonnes, 10 million tonnes, and 25 million tonnes. The highest concentrations of excess nitrogen appear in the darkest brown band, exceeding 25 million tonnes, which covers China and India. These two nations stand apart from the rest of the world in terms of magnitude. A second tier of high values, falling in the 10 million to 25 million tonne range, includes the United States, Brazil, and Pakistan. Western Europe shows a cluster of countries including France, Germany, and the United Kingdom that sit in the 1 million to 10 million tonne band. In contrast, vast geographic areas display significantly lower levels or no data. Most of sub-Saharan Africa, including nations like Niger, Chad, and Botswana, along with Russia and Canada, fall into the lowest visible bands or are marked as having no data. Australia and Mexico appear in the intermediate orange shades, indicating values between 1 million and 10 million tonnes. The visual distribution places the heaviest nitrogen surplus in South and East Asia and North America, while large portions of the Southern Hemisphere and northern latitudes show minimal surplus relative to the global leaders. The data shows a concentration of extreme nitrogen loss in a small number of highly populous and agriculturally intensive regions.
